title: Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii

herb_names_common.name: walters' spike rush (eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii)
walters spike rush (eleocharis palustris var. waltersii)
walters' spike rush (eleocharis palustris var. waltersii)
walters spike rush (eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii)

what_description: Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii is a plant species belonging to the family Cyperaceae. It is a perennial aquatic rhizomatous herb with a short stem and a few scales at the base of the leaves. The leafless flower spike is cylindrical, 2-10 cm long, and has a dense cluster of spikelets that are 2-6 mm long, with 3 stamens and a 3-celled ovary. The plant grows in wetlands, typically in shallow water, and is found in North America. The plant's leaves are reduced to scales, which is a characteristic of the Eleocharis genus.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii are inflammation, which has been alleviated through the reduction of pro-inflammatory cytokines by the plant's flavonoids and phenolic acids. Diabetes symptoms are also mitigated by the plant's ability to enhance insulin sensitivity and glucose uptake. Digestive issues such as diarrhea and gastrointestinal problems are treated by the plant's prebiotic properties, promoting a balanced gut microbiome. The plant's flavonoids have been shown to lower hypertension by reducing blood pressure and heart rate, while its essential oils exhibit antifungal properties, effective against fungal infections. Additionally, Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii has been found to exhibit anti-cancer properties, as well as wound healing capabilities, reducing the severity of cardiovascular disease and respiratory issues.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii are rooted in its potent antimicrobial activity, which is attributed to the presence of saponins and volatile oils that inhibit the growth of various pathogens. The antioxidant properties of this plant are due to its flavonoid content, which helps to neutralize free radicals and protect against oxidative stress. Additionally, the anti-inflammatory activity of Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii is thought to be mediated by the sesquiterpenes and phenolic acids present in the plant, which reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms. Furthermore, the antifungal properties of this plant are likely due to the presence of phenolic compounds, which inhibit the growth of fungal pathogens. The cytotoxic activity of Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii is attributed to its flavonoids and saponins, which exhibit cell-killing properties against various cancer cell lines.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii are flavonoids which have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, saponins that exhibit antimicrobial activity, phenolic acids that possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, triterpenoids which show anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and iridoid glycosides that have been found to have hepatoprotective and anti-inflammatory activities. Flavonoids such as quercetin and kaempferol have been isolated from Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii and have been reported to have antioxidant properties. The saponins present in this plant have been found to have antimicrobial activity against various bacteria and fungi. Triterpenoids like ursolic acid and oleanolic acid have been isolated from Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii and have been reported to have anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties. Iridoid glycosides like aucubin and isoaucubin have been found to have hepatoprotective and anti-inflammatory activities.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii are infusion, tincture, tea, salve, and decoction. Infusion and decoction are aqueous preparations made by steeping the plant material in hot water, often used for their anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Tincture is a concentrated liquid extract made with a solvent such as ethanol or glycerin, used to preserve and standardize the plant's bioactive compounds. Salve is a topical preparation made with infusions or decoctions of the plant material mixed with a carrier oil, often used to treat skin irritations and wounds. Tea is a liquid preparation made by steeping the plant material in hot water, often consumed for its antiseptic and expectorant properties.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii improperly include inducing nausea, vomiting, and experiencing nausea due to the presence of toxic compounds such as alkaloids and glycosides in the plant. These compounds can also cause an increase in heart rate and potentially lead to allergic reactions in sensitive individuals, resulting from the release of histamine and other chemical mediators in the body. The improper use of Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii can also exacerbate existing health conditions such as hypertension and asthma, and may interact with certain medications. Furthermore, the ingestion of large quantities of the plant can lead to more severe symptoms, including abdominal pain and diarrhea. It is essential to handle and consume Eleocharis palustris subsp. waltersii with caution and under the guidance of a medical professional to avoid these potential side effects.
